title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: Six paragraphs have been removed from the text. Choose the correct paragraph (A-G) to fill each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.title: The Price of Brilliance: Rethinking High Intelligence
content.text: For as long as we have studied the human mind, we have equated high intelligence with success, happiness, and social status. Our education systems are designed to identify 'gifted' children and fast-track them to leadership roles. However, recent psychological studies are beginning to suggest that a high IQ might come with its own set of unique burdens. || (41) || [41] This 'curse of the gifted' often manifests as a hyper-awareness of the world's problems [/41]. While an average person might be able to compartmentalize global crises or personal failures, highly intelligent individuals often find themselves unable to 'switch off' their analytical minds, leading to chronic anxiety. || (42) || [42] Beyond this emotional toll, there is the problem of 'social friction' [/42]. Being significantly more intelligent than your peers can lead to a sense of profound isolation. It can be difficult to find common ground when your internal world is operating on a fundamentally different frequency from those around you. || (43) || [43] For many, this leads to a deliberate 'masking' of their intelligence [/43]. In an effort to fit in, highly gifted people may hide their true interests or suppress their vocabulary, a process that is mentally exhausting and can eventually lead to an identity crisis. || (44) || [44] However, the challenges are not just social; they are also cognitive [/44]. Highly intelligent people are often prone to 'over-thinking' simple decisions. Because they can see twenty different consequences for every action, they can become paralyzed by the sheer number of variables, a phenomenon known as 'analysis paralysis.' || (45) || [45] Interestingly, this intelligence doesn't always translate into better life choices [/45]. In fact, some studies show that people with high IQs are just as likely—if not more likely—to fall for cognitive biases. They are so good at 'rationalizing' their mistakes that they can convince themselves they are right even when the evidence says otherwise. || (46) || [46] Despite these pitfalls, intelligence remains a powerful tool [/46]. The key, psychologists suggest, is not to lower our expectations of the gifted, but to provide them with the emotional intelligence (EQ) needed to manage their high-powered minds. Brilliance, it seems, is only an advantage if you know how to steer it.

paragraphs.A: [42] This is because the brain's 'default mode' is constantly scanning for patterns and anomalies [/42]. What others see as a simple event, the gifted mind sees as a complex web of cause and effect, making it impossible to enjoy the moment without over-analyzing it.
paragraphs.B: [41] It appears that the very traits that allow for deep insight can also lead to deep distress [/41]. The ability to process vast amounts of information means that the 'noise' of the world—the suffering, the injustice, and the risks—is felt more acutely.
paragraphs.C: [44] This is why many geniuses are famously indecisive in their personal lives [/44]. While they can solve complex mathematical equations in seconds, choosing what to have for dinner can become a grueling mental exercise in optimization.
paragraphs.D: [43] This 'chameleon effect' is particularly common in school environments [/43]. Children quickly learn that being the 'smartest person in the room' can make them a target for resentment, so they choose to blend into the background instead.
paragraphs.E: [45] This is often referred to as the 'intelligence trap' [/45]. It occurs because a high IQ allows a person to build more complex arguments to defend their existing beliefs, making them less open to changing their minds.
paragraphs.F: [46] This requires a shift in how we define 'potential' [/46]. We need to realize that a high IQ is like a high-performance engine; it needs a sophisticated cooling system and a skilled driver to prevent it from overheating.
paragraphs.G: High intelligence is also strongly linked to creativity. Many of history's greatest artists and scientists were able to see connections that others missed, leading to breakthroughs that changed the course of civilization.

questions.explanation: B conecta el 'distrés' profundo con la capacidad de procesar información, siguiendo la idea de que el IQ alto trae sus propias cargas.
A explica por qué la mente analítica no puede 'desconectarse' (escaneo constante de patrones), lo cual causa la ansiedad mencionada.
D introduce el 'chameleon effect' como ejemplo del 'masking' o camuflaje social mencionado en el texto principal.
C ilustra la 'analysis paralysis' con el ejemplo de la indecisión en la vida personal de los genios.
E define el 'intelligence trap', explicando por qué la gente inteligente es buena racionalizando sus propios errores.
F utiliza la metáfora del 'motor de alto rendimiento' para explicar la necesidad de gestionar la inteligencia con EQ.
